era_context: The mid-18th century marked the zenith of the European Enlightenment, with ambitious projects of systematic knowledge production, intensifying colonial rivalries between Britain, France, Spain, and Portugal, and the consolidation of powerful absolutist monarchies. The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor presided over one of the largest and most prosperous empires in the world, the Mughal Empire continued its fragmentation into regional successor states, and the Ottoman Empire under Mahmud I experienced relative stability after the turmoil of the Tulip Period. In Africa, powerful kingdoms such as Ashanti and Dahomey flourished in West Africa, while the Atlantic slave trade reached devastating scale. The Americas were shaped by expanding European settlement, indigenous resistance, and growing tensions over imperial economic regulation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (interior kingdoms such as Lunda, Luba, Buganda, and Great Zimbabwe successor polities are poorly documented in European-language sources for this year)
Southeast Asia (Burmese, Siamese, and Vietnamese court records underrepresented in English-language historiography)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (pre-European-contact oral traditions)
Central Asian khanates beyond Qing frontier zones

events.title: Treaty of Madrid between Spain and Portugal
events.description: Signed on 13 January 1750, the Treaty of Madrid between Ferdinand VI of Spain and John V of Portugal sought to replace the obsolete Treaty of Tordesillas (1494) by establishing new boundaries between Spanish and Portuguese territories in South America. Portugal gained control of much of the Rio Grande do Sul region and the interior of modern Brazil, while Spain received Colonia del Sacramento on the Rio de la Plata. The treaty required the relocation of seven Jesuit missions (the Guarani Missions) and their indigenous populations, which led directly to the Guarani War (1754-1756). Though the treaty was annulled by the Treaty of El Pardo in 1761, it established the general territorial outlines that would shape the borders of modern Brazil.
key_figures: Jose de Carvajal y Lancaster (Spanish chief negotiator)
Tomas da Silva Teles (Portuguese ambassador to Spain)

sources.name: Treaty of Madrid (1750) — original diplomatic text preserved in Spanish and Portuguese royal archives
David G. Marley, Wars of the Americas: A Chronology of Armed Conflict in the Western Hemisphere (2008)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic treaty with precise date, preserved in state archives and widely attested in contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Death of Johann Sebastian Bach
events.description: Johann Sebastian Bach died on 28 July 1750 in Leipzig at the age of 65, likely from complications following eye surgery performed by the itinerant oculist John Taylor. Bach had served as Thomaskantor and Director Musices in Leipzig since 1723, producing an enormous body of sacred and secular works including the Mass in B minor, the St Matthew Passion, The Well-Tempered Clavier, and the Art of Fugue (left incomplete at his death). Though his reputation declined in the decades immediately following his death in favour of the galant style, his works were preserved by his sons and students, and his eventual rediscovery in the 19th century — particularly through Mendelssohn's 1829 revival of the St Matthew Passion — established him as one of the foundational figures of Western music.
key_figures: Johann Sebastian Bach (composer, Thomaskantor of Leipzig)
Anna Magdalena Bach (wife, who preserved manuscripts)
John Taylor (oculist who operated on Bach's eyes)

sources.name: Johann Nikolaus Forkel, Ueber Johann Sebastian Bachs Leben, Kunst und Kunstwerke (1802) — earliest biography, based on testimony of Bach's sons
Christoph Wolff, Johann Sebastian Bach: The Learned Musician (2000)
Leipzig parish death records, 1750

events.certainty_note: Death recorded in Leipzig parish records; date and circumstances well attested by family and contemporaries.

events.title: Death of John V of Portugal and accession of Jose I
events.description: John V of Portugal died on 31 July 1750 after a long illness, having reigned since 1706. His reign had been defined by the enormous wealth flowing from Brazilian gold mines, which he spent lavishly on religious patronage (notably the Mafra Palace). His son Jose I succeeded him and almost immediately appointed Sebastiao Jose de Carvalho e Melo (later Marquis of Pombal) as Secretary of State. Pombal would become the effective ruler of Portugal for the next 27 years, implementing sweeping Enlightenment-influenced reforms including the suppression of the Jesuits, reorganisation of the economy, and the reconstruction of Lisbon after the 1755 earthquake.
key_figures: Sebastiao Jose de Carvalho e Melo, Marquis of Pombal (chief minister)

sources.name: Kenneth Maxwell, Pombal: Paradox of the Enlightenment (1995)
Contemporary Portuguese court records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Royal succession well documented in court records, diplomatic dispatches, and multiple contemporary chronicles.

events.title: British Iron Act restricts colonial manufacturing
events.description: The British Parliament passed the Iron Act in 1750, which encouraged the importation of pig iron and bar iron from the American colonies to Britain (by removing import duties) while prohibiting the erection of new slitting mills, steel furnaces, and plating forges in the colonies. The act exemplified mercantilist policy: colonies were to supply raw materials while the mother country retained the monopoly on finished goods manufacturing. In practice, enforcement was lax and colonial ironworking continued to expand, but the act contributed to growing colonial resentment over imperial economic restrictions that would eventually fuel revolutionary sentiment.

sources.name: Iron Act (23 Geo. II, c. 29, 1750) — parliamentary statute text
Arthur Cecil Bining, British Regulation of the Colonial Iron Industry (1933)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary statute with precise text preserved in official records.

events.title: Rousseau wins the Dijon Academy prize for the First Discourse
events.description: In 1750, Jean-Jacques Rousseau won the prize of the Academie de Dijon for his Discours sur les sciences et les arts (Discourse on the Sciences and Arts), which argued that the progress of civilisation had corrupted rather than improved human morals. The essay, published in January 1751, made Rousseau famous overnight and launched his career as one of the most influential and controversial thinkers of the Enlightenment. His argument that natural simplicity was morally superior to civilised refinement prefigured the themes he would develop in the Discourse on Inequality (1755) and The Social Contract (1762), works that profoundly influenced the French Revolution and modern political thought.

sources.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Discours sur les sciences et les arts (1751) — the work itself
Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Les Confessions (written 1765-1770, published posthumously 1782) — describes the moment of inspiration and prize
Leo Damrosch, Jean-Jacques Rousseau: Restless Genius (2005)

events.certainty_note: Prize awarded in 1750; the essay was published in early 1751. Dates confirmed by Academy records and Rousseau's own correspondence.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor consolidates Qing control over Tibet
events.description: By 1750, the Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor exercised significant political control over Tibet through its resident ambans (imperial commissioners). In November 1750, a crisis erupted when the Tibetan regent Gyurme Namgyal was assassinated by the ambans Fuchin and Labdon after he was suspected of plotting to break free of Qing authority. A Tibetan mob then killed the ambans in retaliation. The Qianlong Emperor dispatched a military expedition to restore order, and in the aftermath restructured Tibetan governance by establishing the Kashag (council of ministers) and strengthening the Dalai Lama's temporal authority while ensuring ultimate Qing suzerainty through reformed amban oversight.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty) — official court chronicles
Luciano Petech, China and Tibet in the Early XVIIIth Century (1972)
Melvyn Goldstein, A History of Modern Tibet (1989)

events.certainty_note: Events documented in Qing court records, Tibetan chronicles, and Jesuit missionary reports. Precise dates established from multiple independent sources.

events.title: Ahmad Shah Durrani raids northern India
events.description: Ahmad Shah Durrani, founder of the Durrani Empire and ruler of Afghanistan, continued his series of invasions into the declining Mughal Empire's northern territories around 1750. His campaigns targeted the Punjab and the wealthy regions around Delhi and Lahore, exploiting the Mughal Empire's inability to defend its frontiers as regional powers competed for dominance. These raids were part of a broader pattern of Durrani incursions that would culminate in the sack of Delhi (1757) and the decisive Third Battle of Panipat (1761). The raids accelerated Mughal decline and intensified the power vacuum that the Marathas, Sikhs, and eventually the British East India Company would compete to fill.
key_figures: Ahmad Shah Durrani (founder of the Durrani Empire)

sources.name: Tarikh-i-Ahmad Shahi by Mahmud al-Husayni — court chronicle of Ahmad Shah Durrani
Jos J. L. Gommans, The Rise of the Indo-Afghan Empire, c. 1710-1780 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Ahmad Shah's campaigns in this period are well attested, though the precise chronology of individual raids in 1750 versus adjacent years varies between sources.

events.title: Expansion of the Maratha Confederacy under the Peshwa
events.description: By 1750, the Maratha Confederacy under Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao had emerged as the dominant indigenous power in the Indian subcontinent, with effective control extending far beyond its Deccan heartland. The Marathas collected chauth (tribute) across vast territories and their military chiefs — the Scindias, Holkars, Gaekwads, and Bhonsles — governed semi-autonomous domains from Malwa to Orissa. This period represented the high-water mark of Maratha expansion before the catastrophic defeat at Panipat in 1761. The Maratha rise occurred in the context of accelerating Mughal fragmentation and increasing competition with the Durrani Afghans, the Nizam of Hyderabad, and European trading companies.

sources.name: Peshwa Daftar (Maratha administrative records preserved at Pune)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600-1818 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Maratha political dominance in this period is well attested through administrative records, correspondence, and British East India Company reports.

events.title: Samuel Johnson begins The Rambler periodical
events.description: On 20 March 1750, Samuel Johnson published the first issue of The Rambler, a twice-weekly periodical essay series that ran until 14 March 1752, producing 208 issues. Written almost entirely by Johnson himself, the essays covered literary criticism, moral philosophy, human psychology, and social observation in a distinctive prose style that established Johnson as the foremost English literary figure of his age. The Rambler solidified Johnson's intellectual authority and readership in the years before his landmark Dictionary of the English Language (1755), which would further cement his dominance over English letters.

sources.name: The Rambler (1750-1752) — the periodical itself, preserved in contemporary print runs
James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson (1791)

events.certainty_note: Publication dates confirmed from printed issues and contemporary publishing records.

events.title: French fortification of the Ohio Valley frontier
events.description: By 1750, France was actively consolidating its claim to the Ohio Valley, a vast territory connecting its possessions in Canada and Louisiana that was also claimed by the British colonies of Virginia and Pennsylvania. The Marquis de la Galissoniere, governor of New France, dispatched Celoron de Blainville in 1749 to bury lead plates asserting French sovereignty along the Ohio River, and by 1750 French policy was shifting toward constructing a chain of forts to enforce this claim. These actions directly antagonized British colonial expansion, Virginian land speculators (including the Ohio Company), and indigenous nations such as the Shawnee, Delaware, and Mingo. The escalating rivalry would ignite the French and Indian War (1754-1763), which merged into the global Seven Years' War.
key_figures: Marquis de la Galissoniere (Governor of New France)
Christopher Gist (Ohio Company agent exploring the region)

sources.name: Celoron de Blainville's journal (1749) and subsequent French colonial correspondence
Fred Anderson, Crucible of War: The Seven Years' War and the Fate of Empire in British North America (2000)

events.certainty_note: French frontier policy well documented in colonial archives; Celoron's expedition and lead plates are attested by physical evidence (recovered plates) and written records.

events.title: Karim Khan Zand rises to power in Persia
events.description: Following the assassination of Nadir Shah in 1747 and the subsequent collapse of Afsharid authority, Persia descended into a multi-sided civil war among competing claimants. By 1750, Karim Khan of the Zand tribe had emerged as one of the leading contenders for power, controlling significant territory in southern and central Iran. He would eventually defeat his rivals and establish the Zand dynasty, ruling from Shiraz as Vakil al-Ro'aya (Advocate of the People) — notably refusing the title of Shah. His reign (effective from the early 1750s until his death in 1779) brought relative stability and prosperity to Persia after decades of warfare.

sources.name: John R. Perry, Karim Khan Zand: A History of Iran, 1747-1779 (1979)
Tarikh-e Giti-Gosha by Mirza Mohammad Sadiq Musavi — Persian chronicle of the period

events.certainty_note: The Zand rise to power was a gradual process; attributing specific territorial control to exactly 1750 involves some imprecision, though the general period is well established.

events.title: Diderot publishes the Prospectus for the Encyclopedie
events.description: In November 1750, Denis Diderot published the Prospectus for the Encyclopedie, ou dictionnaire raisonne des sciences, des arts et des metiers, a detailed description of the planned work that served as both an intellectual manifesto and a subscription drive. The Prospectus outlined the project's scope — a comprehensive, rationally organised compendium of all human knowledge — and attracted over 2,000 subscribers. This laid the commercial and intellectual groundwork for the first volume's publication in June 1751, launching what would become the defining publishing project of the Enlightenment.

sources.name: Denis Diderot, Prospectus de l'Encyclopedie (November 1750) — the document itself
Robert Darnton, The Business of Enlightenment: A Publishing History of the Encyclopedie (1979)

events.certainty_note: The Prospectus is a surviving document with a clear publication date, confirmed by publishing records and correspondence.

events.title: Benjamin Franklin's experiments on electricity
events.description: By 1750, Benjamin Franklin had been conducting his groundbreaking experiments on electricity in Philadelphia for several years and was corresponding with Peter Collinson of the Royal Society in London about his findings. In 1750, Franklin proposed his famous experiment to prove the electrical nature of lightning using a pointed iron rod (later adapted into the kite experiment of 1752). His letters to Collinson were compiled and published in London in 1751 as Experiments and Observations on Electricity, which brought Franklin international scientific fame. His work established fundamental concepts including positive and negative electrical charges, the conservation of charge, and the practical invention of the lightning rod.
key_figures: Peter Collinson (Fellow of the Royal Society, Franklin's correspondent)

sources.name: Benjamin Franklin, letters to Peter Collinson (1747-1750), published as Experiments and Observations on Electricity (1751)
I. Bernard Cohen, Benjamin Franklin's Science (1990)

events.certainty_note: Franklin's letters to Collinson are dated and preserved; the progression of his experiments is well documented.

events.title: Tokugawa Shogunate under Ieshige; relative peace in Japan
events.description: Japan in 1750 was governed by Tokugawa Ieshige, the ninth Tokugawa shogun (r. 1745-1760), during the long era of internal peace and strict seclusion (sakoku) that characterised the middle Edo period. Ieshige's reign was notable for his reliance on his chamberlain Ooka Tadamitsu, who wielded considerable power. The period saw continued urbanisation, the flourishing of merchant culture in Osaka and Edo, expansion of agricultural productivity, and the growth of a money economy that increasingly strained the rice-based stipend system of the samurai class. Japan's population had stabilised at roughly 30 million after the growth of the early Edo period.

sources.name: Tokugawa Jikki (Veritable Records of the Tokugawa) — official shogunal chronicle
Conrad Totman, Early Modern Japan (1993)

events.certainty_note: Tokugawa administrative records are extensive and well preserved; the political and economic conditions of mid-Edo Japan are thoroughly documented.

events.title: King Yeongjo's reforms continue in Joseon Korea
events.description: King Yeongjo of Joseon (r. 1724-1776) continued his policy of tangpyeong (impartiality) in 1750, attempting to balance the power of the competing factional parties (Noron, Soron, Namin, Bukin) that had destabilised the Korean court for generations. In 1750, Yeongjo promulgated the Gyunyeokbeop (Equalized Tax Law), a significant fiscal reform that reduced the military cloth tax burden on commoners by half and compensated for the revenue shortfall through new levies on the yangban elite and other sources. This was one of the most important social welfare reforms of the Joseon dynasty, reducing the crushing tax burden that had provoked widespread popular discontent.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Dynasty) — official court records, UNESCO Memory of the World
JaHyun Kim Haboush, The Confucian Kingship in Korea: Yongjo and the Politics of Sagacity (1988)

events.certainty_note: The Joseon Annals provide detailed daily records of court business; the Equalized Tax Law is precisely dated and its provisions are well documented.

events.title: Ashanti Empire at its height under Opoku Ware I
events.description: The Ashanti Empire under Asantehene Opoku Ware I (r. c. 1720-1750) reached its greatest territorial extent by mid-century, controlling much of modern Ghana and dominating the gold and slave trades of the Gold Coast hinterland. Opoku Ware I died around 1750 (some sources give 1742, others as late as 1750), having expanded Ashanti dominion over the Dagomba, Gonja, and other northern states. The empire's wealth was built on gold mining, trade networks, and the sale of war captives to European merchants at coastal forts. The sophisticated Ashanti political system, with its centralised bureaucracy, provincial governance, and the symbolic authority of the Golden Stool, represented one of the most powerful state formations in pre-colonial West Africa.

sources.name: Ivor Wilks, Asante in the Nineteenth Century (1975)
Dutch West India Company trading records from Elmina

events.certainty_note: The death date of Opoku Ware I is debated among historians (ranging from 1742 to 1750); Ashanti political chronology in this period relies partly on oral tradition reconciled with European trading records.

events.title: Ottoman Empire in a period of relative stability under Mahmud I
events.description: The Ottoman Empire in 1750 was in a period of relative peace under Sultan Mahmud I (r. 1730-1754), who had come to power following the Patrona Halil Revolt that ended the Tulip Period. After the Treaty of Belgrade (1739) ended wars with both Austria and Russia on terms favourable to the Ottomans, the empire enjoyed a decade of stability. However, structural challenges persisted: provincial notables (ayan) were accumulating autonomous power, the Janissary corps was increasingly undisciplined, and the empire's military technology was falling behind European developments. The period saw modest architectural and cultural patronage in Constantinople but no major institutional reforms.

sources.name: Subhi Mehmed Efendi, Subhi Tarihi — Ottoman court chronicle covering this period
Caroline Finkel, Osman's Dream: The Story of the Ottoman Empire, 1300-1923 (2005)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman court records and European diplomatic dispatches provide consistent documentation of this period.

events.title: Atlantic slave trade at peak volume
events.description: The mid-18th century represented one of the peak periods of the transatlantic slave trade. The Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database estimates that approximately 80,000 enslaved Africans were transported across the Atlantic annually in the years around 1750, the majority destined for sugar plantations in the Caribbean and Brazil. British, Portuguese, French, and Dutch vessels dominated the trade, sourcing captives primarily from West and West Central Africa. The scale of forced deportation had devastating demographic, social, and political consequences for African societies, fuelling wars of capture, reorienting economies toward the slave trade, and depopulating vast regions. The human cost of this commerce is incalculable.

sources.name: Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org) — compiled from shipping records, port logs, and colonial administrative documents
David Eltis and David Richardson, Atlas of the Transatlantic Slave Trade (2010)

events.certainty_note: Aggregate volume estimates are based on extensive shipping records; individual voyage data is well documented for this period, though total figures involve some statistical extrapolation for unrecorded voyages.

events.title: Empress Elizabeth of Russia builds the Winter Palace
events.description: In 1750, the construction of the current Winter Palace in Saint Petersburg, commissioned by Empress Elizabeth of Russia and designed by the Italian architect Bartolomeo Francesco Rastrelli, was in its planning and early stages (construction formally began in 1754). Elizabeth's reign (1741-1762) was characterised by lavish court culture modelled on Versailles, the founding of Moscow University (1755), and the consolidation of Russia's position as a major European power. Russia maintained alliances with Austria and France and was positioning itself for what would become the Seven Years' War (1756-1763), which would dramatically expand Russian influence in European affairs.
key_figures: Mikhail Lomonosov (polymath, active in Russian Academy of Sciences)

sources.name: Russian Imperial court records and construction commissions
Simon Dixon, Catherine the Great (2009) — provides context for Elizabethan court culture

events.certainty_note: Elizabethan court activities and Rastrelli's commission are documented in court records and diplomatic reports; the Winter Palace construction timeline is established.

events.title: Alivardi Khan defends Bengal against Maratha incursions
events.description: Nawab Alivardi Khan of Bengal (r. 1740-1756) continued to defend his wealthy province against Maratha Bargir raids that had devastated the region since the 1740s. By 1750, Alivardi had reached a tenuous accommodation with the Marathas, agreeing to pay chauth tribute in exchange for a cessation of raids. Bengal under Alivardi was the wealthiest province of the Mughal Empire and a major trading partner of the European companies — especially the British East India Company at Calcutta. Alivardi's death in 1756 and the succession of his young grandson Siraj ud-Daulah would trigger the crisis that led to the Battle of Plassey (1757) and the beginning of British territorial rule in India.
key_figures: Raghoji I Bhonsle (Maratha Bhonsle chief who led raids into Bengal)

sources.name: Ghulam Husain Khan Tabatabai, Siyar ul-Mutakhkharin (late 18th century) — Persian-language history of Bengal
British East India Company factory records from Fort William, Calcutta

events.certainty_note: Alivardi Khan's conflicts with the Marathas are documented in both Mughal-Bengali chronicles and European trading company records.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of contestation or nuance bear noting: (1) The death date of Ashanti ruler Opoku Ware I is genuinely uncertain — Ivor Wilks places it around 1742, while other traditions suggest dates as late as 1750. The event is included here with an 'approximate' certainty marker, but readers should be aware this may belong to an earlier year. (2) The significance of the Iron Act is sometimes overstated in American revolutionary narratives as evidence of oppressive mercantilist policy; in practice, the act was poorly enforced and colonial iron production actually increased after 1750. Its importance lies more in the principle of metropolitan control than in its practical economic impact. (3) Rousseau's First Discourse is sometimes presented as a rejection of the Enlightenment; modern scholarship (e.g., Mark Hulliung, The Autocritique of Enlightenment, 1994) reads it more carefully as an internal critique that shared many Enlightenment assumptions while challenging others. (4) The characterisation of Ahmad Shah Durrani's campaigns in exactly 1750 involves some chronological imprecision — sources differ on the timing of specific raids within the 1748-1752 period. (5) The claim that Bach's eye surgery was a direct cause of death is traditional but not medically certain; modern scholars note his health had been declining for years and the precise cause of death remains debated.
historiographic_note: The year 1750 falls in a period of rich documentation for Europe, the Ottoman Empire, Qing China, Mughal India, and the Atlantic world, with extensive diplomatic correspondence, court chronicles, trading company records, and early periodical press coverage. However, significant biases shape the record: European sources dominate global narratives; colonial administrative records reflect the priorities and prejudices of colonial officials; African and Southeast Asian history depends more heavily on archaeological evidence and oral traditions reconciled with external (often European) written sources. The Qing and Joseon archives are comprehensive but remain underrepresented in English-language historiography. Indigenous American voices are mediated almost entirely through European observers. The mid-18th century is also a period where Enlightenment self-documentation creates an illusion of more rapid intellectual change than may have been experienced by ordinary people, who constituted the overwhelming majority of the world's population.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Madrid sought to supersede the obsolete Treaty of Tordesillas boundary between Spanish and Portuguese territories
Forced relocation of Guarani mission populations triggered the Guarani War (1754-1756)
Pombal's appointment under Jose I positioned him to lead the Lisbon earthquake reconstruction and broader reforms
Durrani raids into northern India escalated toward the decisive Third Battle of Panipat (1761)
Maratha expansion into northern India brought them into conflict with the Durrani Empire, culminating at Panipat
French-British rivalry over the Ohio Valley directly ignited the French and Indian War in 1754
The Prospectus generated subscriptions and set the stage for Volume I of the Encyclopedie in 1751
Rousseau's First Discourse launched the intellectual trajectory culminating in The Social Contract (1762)
Alivardi Khan's management of Bengal set the stage for the succession crisis and Battle of Plassey after his death in 1756
The 1750 Lhasa crisis was part of the broader Qing consolidation of suzerainty over Tibet throughout the 18th century
